steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Regroup the expression: $x^2 - c^2 - 2cd - d^2 = x^2 - (c^2 + 2cd + d^2)$.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: Recognize that $c^2 + 2cd + d^2 = (c+d)^2$. Since $a = c + d$, substitute to get $x^2 - a^2$.

steps.title: Step 3
steps.content: Apply the difference of squares formula: $x^2 - a^2 = (x+a)(x-a)$.

choices.explanation:
• Expanding: $(x+a)^2 = x^2 + 2ax + a^2$. This has a $+2ax$ term and $+a^2$, neither of which appears in the original.
  • The original expression has $-a^2$, not $+a^2$.
  • Eliminated because the sign of $a^2$ and the middle term are both wrong.

• Expanding: $(x-a)^2 = x^2 - 2ax + a^2$. This introduces a $-2ax$ cross term and keeps $+a^2$.
  • The original expression has no $x$-linear term and has $-a^2$, not $+a^2$.
  • Eliminated because both the sign of $a^2$ and the extra cross term are wrong.

• This is the difference of squares: $(x+a)(x-a) = x^2 - a^2$.
  • We showed $x^2 - c^2 - 2cd - d^2 = x^2 - (c+d)^2 = x^2 - a^2$, which matches exactly.
  • That matches $(x + a) \times (x - a)$, so C is correct.

• This expression contains $-ax$, a cross term in $x$ and $a$ that does not appear in the original.
  • No manipulation of $x^2 - c^2 - 2cd - d^2$ produces a linear-in-$x$ term.
  • Eliminated because the $-ax$ term is extraneous.
